competition.narrative: Competition is CONFIRMED and comes from two sources: Coupa (the 2-year incumbent full S2P platform) and organizational inertia / Do Nothing. Coupa is the STRUCTURAL competitor: Calvin stated (15 Sep 2025) 'we are using Coupa already… it's been one and a half years' and confirmed (13 Mar 2026) Coupa covers full S2P, both direct and indirect materials. Coupa is deeply operationally entrenched — 18+ months of use, full upstream and downstream deployment, and user adoption already in place. Guillaume acknowledged (15 Sep) 'if you are more or less happy with Coupa, I don't really see the opportunity for us to move forward' and positioned Zycus's AI maturity as the WEDGE: 'the market clearly recognized that Zycus is more mature in AI than any other vendors in the market, including Coupa… that's what customers are telling us at this moment.' The buyer's question (13 Mar: 'in the span of 6 months, anything changed? Anything new?') signals they are BENCHMARKING Zycus AI capabilities against Coupa's evolving AI roadmap — this is an active comparison, not a sole-source evaluation. Guillaume also stated (15 Sep) Zycus Anna/Intake cannot integrate with third-party platforms like Coupa ('my response is simple but clear: no'), which means the buyer must choose to the relevant stakeholder as a standalone module OR continue with Coupa alone — there is no hybrid path. The second competitor is DO NOTHING / DEFER: the 58-day silence, 10% rep confidence, and undelivered buyer commitments (demo, use-case list) suggest the buyer may lack urgency or internal sponsorship to act. The S/4HANA migration (2027) is a potential catalyst but not a hard dependency — the buyer could defer the Anna decision indefinitely. Our wedge is AI maturity and native agentic architecture; our risk is Coupa entrenchment and the buyer's inability to articulate a standalone Anna use case that justifies the integration complexity and political lift of adding a second vendor.

identify_pain.narrative: The buyer's pain is CONFIRMED and comes from three operational sources, all owned by Calvin Sidharta (Head of CI, Contract & Supplier Mgmt) and his Continuous Improvement team (Arjun, mentioned 15 Sep). First, material master-data inconsistency: Calvin stated (15 Sep 2025) 'we are currently facing some issues in regards to our master data, our material master… normalizing the data following the UNSPSC standard' — this was the original PSS inquiry and remains unresolved. Second, S/4HANA migration complexity: the buyer is migrating from the relevant stakeholder to S/4HANA in staggered phases starting 2027 (13 Mar 2026), and the migration is forcing a review of all procurement systems to avoid 'garbage in, garbage out' (Calvin, 15 Sep). Third, shareholder mandate for AI adoption: Calvin stated (13 Mar 2026) 'the push from our shareholders is to embed agentic AI to our S2P processes' — this is a top-down strategic directive, not a bottom-up operational pain, which elevates the initiative but also introduces political risk (if the unnamed shareholder sponsor disengages, the initiative may stall). The pain is real, confirmed, and owned by Calvin's team, but it has not been quantified (no cost/impact numbers), and the Economic Buyer who would fund the solution has not been engaged. The pain is sufficient to justify technical exploration (which is where the deal sits today), but not yet sufficient to drive a commercial decision without EB engagement and a quantified business case.

decision_process.narrative: The buyer's decision process is SEQUENTIAL and gated by the S/4HANA migration timeline (2027 rollout in staggered phases, Calvin 13 Mar 2026), but the procurement-layer decision steps are unclear. The call sequence shows: (1) initial PSS event inquiry (Sep 2025, master-data focus), (2) technical intro call (15 Sep 2025, Coupa context, direct/indirect scope, AI positioning), (3) second intro / Anna-focus call (13 Mar 2026, shareholder AI mandate, demo request), (4) planned Anna demo (never scheduled, buyer-side commitment undelivered). Beyond the demo, the buyer has not articulated approval gates, procurement committee, legal/InfoSec review, or CFO sign-off steps. Calvin mentioned 'group procurement' vs 'shared service procurement' (15 Sep) and that the shared-service team (where Calvin sits) supports 14 spend categories, suggesting a matrixed decision structure, but he did not name who approves new technology or how Anna would be piloted/procured. The S/4 migration is a PARALLEL track (2027), not a PREREQUISITE — the Anna pilot could proceed independently, but no one has confirmed that. The 58-day silence suggests the buyer either lacks a formal process or is waiting on an internal approval that has stalled. The next move is to ask Calvin to map the approval chain: 'After the demo, what are the next gates — technical validation, EB business-case review, legal/InfoSec, procurement committee, pilot scope approval? Who is involved at each gate, and what is the realistic timeline to a go/no-go decision by end Q3 2026?' Without this map, the deal cannot progress.

decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer's evaluation criteria are IMPLIED from the call sequence but not formally documented or validated. From the 15 Sep 2025 and 13 Mar 2026 calls, the buyer is assessing: (1) whether Anna can normalize material master data to UNSPSC standards (the original PSS inquiry), (2) whether Zycus agentic AI is native and integrated (vs bolt-on like Coupa's third-party AI), (3) whether Anna can handle direct materials and tail-spend automation in an agro-business context (Calvin asked about direct-materials coverage on 15 Sep), (4) data localization and InfoSec requirements for Indonesia deployment (Calvin asked about local customers and data-residency on 13 Mar), and (5) whether Zycus has credible Indonesian or similar-industry references (Calvin explicitly requested Anna customer references — Danone, Dow, Delta — on 13 Mar). These are the INFERRED fit criteria, but the buyer has not shared a formal RFP, BRD, or scorecard, and has not confirmed decision weights or must-haves. The 30-45 minute Anna demo that was promised on 13 Mar and never scheduled would be the forum to validate these criteria — its absence means the criteria remain unconfirmed. The next move is to re-engage Calvin, deliver the demo, and use it to co-create a written decision-criteria checklist that both sides sign off on, converting inference to confirmed fit.

flags: Convert Calvin or Lily from evaluator to advocate by co-building a quantified business case. The pain is confirmed (master-data errors, AI mandate) but qualitative. Work with Calvin to quantify: (1) cost of master-data inconsistencies (procurement cycle time, error rate, ERP migration risk), (2) manual effort in tail-spend negotiation (FTE hours, process cycle time), and (3) shareholder/board credibility of delivering on the AI mandate before the 2027 S/4HANA rollout. Tie the business case to a named finance stakeholder (CFO or FP&A lead) and position Anna as the Phase 1 AI quick-win. A champion emerges when they own the business case and see their career/credibility tied to the outcome.
best_practice_check.summary: This is a technically interesting but commercially stalled early-stage deal with three structural weaknesses: no Economic Buyer engagement (the shareholder sponsor is unnamed and unmapped), no champion (Calvin is receptive but passive and has not driven next steps in 110 days), and Coupa entrenchment (2-year incumbent, full S2P, no clear wedge for Zycus to displace or layer on top without a standalone Anna business case). The 58-day silence and 10% rep confidence indicate the buyer lacks urgency or internal sponsorship to act. The highest-leverage moves are: (1) re-engage Calvin, deliver the Anna demo, and use it to map the Economic Buyer and secure an intro, (2) run a value-scoping workshop to quantify the tail-spend / invoice automation ROI Anna delivers (convert qualitative AI appetite into a quantified business case the EB can approve), and (3) define a no-risk 60-day Anna pilot scope (one business unit, Zycus + BCT delivery, success metrics co-defined) to convert exploration into action. If the buyer cannot commit to EB engagement, a pilot scope, and a decision timeline by end Q3 2026, disqualify and redeploy resources.

competitive_position.summary: Coupa is the entrenched incumbent (2-year deployment, full S2P, direct + indirect) and the PRIMARY current threat. The buyer is benchmarking Zycus AI maturity vs Coupa's evolving AI roadmap but has not moved past technical exploration. Zycus cannot integrate Anna with Coupa, so the buyer must choose to layer Zycus as standalone or continue with Coupa alone — no hybrid path exists. Do Nothing / Defer is the SECONDARY threat: 58-day silence and 10% rep confidence indicate low urgency and possible internal-sponsorship gap. If the buyer cannot articulate a standalone Anna business case or secure EB sign-off, the default outcome is to stay with Coupa and defer. Our wedge is AI maturity (20 years, native agentic architecture, 50+ Anna customers including Danone/Dow/Delta); our risk is Coupa entrenchment and the political/integration complexity of adding a second vendor without a clear champion or EB sponsor.
